title: O privire spre alegerile din Ungaria
summary: The article examines Hungary's obstructionist role within the EU, tracing Viktor Orbán's pro-Russian, Eurasian-oriented foreign policy back to Hungary's historical revisionism rooted in the Treaty of Trianon. It analyzes how Orbán has positioned Hungary at the intersection of Trumpism, Putinism, and Sinophilia, while upcoming elections could see the opposition TISZA party led by Péter Magyar challenge his grip on power.
talking_points: Hungary blocked the EU's €90 billion loan to Ukraine at the March 2026 European Council, demanding Russian oil supplies as a precondition
Orbán's pro-Russian stance is rooted in Hungary's historical revisionism stemming from the Treaty of Trianon and its loss of two-thirds of its territory
Hungary's 95% dependence on Russian gas and the no-bid Rosatom nuclear contract fundamentally shape its foreign policy alignment
Orbán has cultivated a unique geopolitical position at the crossroads of Trumpism, Putinism, Chinese investment, and even pan-Turkic ideology
Opposition party TISZA, led by MEP Péter Magyar, may defeat Orbán according to some polls
concepts: energy dependence as foreign policy lever
related_concepts: Russian influence in EU member states
author: Desk Russie
